sanskrit_devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तपः ।
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह ॥ ९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sañjaya uvāca |
evam uktvā hṛṣīkeśaṁ guḍākeśaḥ paraṁtapaḥ |
na yotsya iti govindam uktvā tūṣṇīṁ babhūva ha || 9 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.009 = MBh_06,024.009). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: to Hṛṣīkeśa (Krishna, master of the senses)

translations.verbatim_quote: Sañjaya said: Having spoken thus, Arjuna, chastiser of enemies, told Kṛṣṇa, "Govinda, I shall not fight," and fell silent.
Sanjay said: Having thus spoken, Gudakesh, that chastiser of enemies, addressed Hrishikesh: 'Govind, I shall not fight,' and became silent.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Dhṛtarāṣṭra must have been very glad to understand that Arjuna was not going to fight and was instead leaving the battlefield for the begging profession. But Sañjaya disappointed him again in relating that Arjuna was competent to kill his enemies (paraṁtapaḥ).

commentaries.source: Multiple commentaries take 9 + 10 as a narrative pair
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: All major traditions (Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, Gaudiya) read 2.9 and 2.10 together as the narrative bridge between Arjuna's case (2.4-2.8) and Krishna's reply (2.11 onward). The choice of epithets is read as significant: Hṛṣīkeśa (lord of the senses) for Krishna, Guḍākeśa (conqueror of sleep) for Arjuna, paraṁtapa (scorcher of foes) — Sanjaya's word-choice signals that Arjuna's declaration of non-fighting is incongruous with who he actually is.

positions.rendering: became silent indeed (the particle 'ha' is emphatic)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators agree on the literal sense. The emphatic particle 'ha' carries weight: Sanjaya is reporting an unusual silence — the fluent, talkative Arjuna of Chapter 1 has now run out of words. The silence is the prerequisite for the teaching that begins at 2.11.

literal_meaning: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus to Hṛṣīkeśa, Guḍākeśa the scorcher of foes — saying 'I will not fight' to Govinda — became silent.
traditional_meaning_consensus: A pure narration verse. Sanjaya reports two facts: Arjuna's final declaration ('I will not fight') and Arjuna's subsequent silence. The verse has no doctrinal content of its own; it functions as scaffolding marking the end of Arjuna's case. The chapter's commentarial tradition takes the verse seriously as a moment of structural significance — the silence is what the teaching at 2.11 rests on — but the verse itself does not teach anything.

remaining_gaps: Shankara/Ramanuja verbatim — narrative verse; commentaries are brief and JS-rendered.
